R package for rapid climate extreme event attribution using the flow analogues approach. Allows reconstruction of surface fields under counterfactual and factual climate…

App



Interactive Shiny app for computing return periods of extreme meteorological events from observational records. Fits statistical distributions and estimates recurrence…

Software



R package for computing synoptic climate classifications and spatial regionalization of environmental variables using gridded atmospheric data. Available on CRAN.

title: About
text:
Short Bio
I am a Beatriu de Pinós Postdoctoral Fellow at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (Earth Sciences Department, Spain) since February 2026. My research focuses on understanding the link between atmospheric dynamics and extreme meteorological events — heatwaves, compound droughts, and extreme precipitation — with particular emphasis on the European Alps and the Mediterranean region. I develop synoptic classification and attribution methods to quantify the role of climate change in shaping these extremes.
I hold a PhD in Geography (Extraordinary Doctorate Prize) from the University of Barcelona, and have held postdoctoral positions at EURAC Research (Italy), Andorra Research+Innovation (Andorra), and the University of Santiago de Compostela (Spain). As Principal Investigator, I have secured competitive funding for the Flash4CastImp project (Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions, Horizon Europe; 2027–2029), the HIPEAtMount project (Province of Bolzano; 2024–2026), and the ATOC project (Barcelona City Council; 2023–2024).
I am the author of synoptReg, an R package for synoptic climate classification widely used in climatological research.


Research Interests

Synoptic climatology and atmospheric circulation
Extreme weather events: heatwaves, droughts, extreme precipitation
Climate change attribution in mountain regions (Alps, Pyrenees)
R programming and open-source tools for climate science



Current Position
Beatriu de Pinós Postdoctoral Fellow · since February 2026

Organizer, public outreach event “Calor extrema a Barcelona: Desafiaments climàtics i solucions urbanes”, Royal Academy of Sciences and Arts of Barcelona



Contact
Feel free to reach out via email or through any of the social links at the bottom of this page.

title: Marc Lemus-Canovas
text:
About
I am a Beatriu de Pinós Postdoctoral Fellow at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (Earth Sciences Department). My research focuses on understanding the link between atmospheric dynamics and extreme meteorological events — compound heat and drought events, extreme precipitation, and their attribution to climate change.

title: Research
text: My research focuses on understanding the link between atmospheric dynamics and extreme meteorological events — heatwaves, compound droughts, and extreme precipitation — with emphasis on the European Alps and Mediterranean region. I develop synoptic classification and attribution methods to quantify the role of climate change in shaping these extremes.

title: Research
text:
Research Projects
As Principal Investigator:

Flash4CastImp — Identification, Monitoring, and Forecasting of Impactful Flash Droughts in Europe. Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA), Horizon Europe. Coordinated by CSIC, Spain. 2027–2029. · CORDIS
HIPEAtMount — Tailored attribution of high-impact precipitation events to anthropogenic climate change in mountain areas. Province of Bolzano/Bozen, Italy. 2024–2026.
ATOC — Heatwave attribution to climate change in Barcelona. Barcelona City Council. 2023–2024.

As Collaborator:

EO4Multihazards — Earth Observation for Multi-Hazard Risk Assessments. European Space Agency. PI: Terzi, S. 2023–2025.
FutureMed — Transdisciplinary network bridging climate science and societal impacts. COST. PI: Khodayar, S. 2023–2027
EXE — Compound dry and warm events in mainland Spain. Spanish Ministry of Science and Innovation. PI: Martin-Vide, J. 2021–2024.
CLICES — Climate of the last century in Spanish mainland. Spanish Ministry of Science and Innovation. PI: Martin-Vide, J. & Lopez-Bustins, J.A. 2018–2020.

Contracts & Transfer:

Synoptic classification of daily surface O₃ concentration in Catalonia — Centre Internacional de Mètodes Numèrics en Enginyeria (FBG311161). 2021–2022

title: Teaching
text:
Workshops
Introduction to R for Climate Scientists
Andorra Research + Innovation, 2022
Half-day workshop covering data import, transformation, and visualization of climate gridded datasets in R using tidyverse and metR.

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
It is possible to read a NetCDF file in a Tidyverse way through the metR package created by Elio Campitelli. This library also contains many useful functions for visualizing and analyzing weather and climate data.
In this tutorial, we will work with a daily grid of 5×5 km of the maximum temperature of the Balearic Islands (Spain) from the STEAD dataset (Serrano-Notivoli 2019).

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(metR)
library(trend)
library(pals) # color palettes

We use GlanceNetCDF() to inspect the file and ReadNetCDF() to read it into a data.frame:

file <- "tmax_bal.nc"

# Inspect the NetCDF structure
variable <- GlanceNetCDF(file = file)
variable

# Read the NetCDF as a data.frame
tx <- ReadNetCDF(
  file = file,
  vars = c(var = names(variable$vars[1])),
  out = "data.frame"
)

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
It is possible to read a NetCDF file in a Tidyverse way through the metR package created by Elio Campitelli. This library also contains many useful functions for visualizing and analyzing weather and climate data.
In this tutorial, we will work with a daily grid of 5×5 km of the maximum temperature of the Balearic Islands (Spain) from the STEAD dataset (Serrano-Notivoli 2019).

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(metR)
library(trend)
library(pals) # color palettes

We use GlanceNetCDF() to inspect the file and ReadNetCDF() to read it into a data.frame:

file <- "tmax_bal.nc"

# Inspect the NetCDF structure
variable <- GlanceNetCDF(file = file)
variable

# Read the NetCDF as a data.frame
tx <- ReadNetCDF(
  file = file,
  vars = c(var = names(variable$vars[1])),
  out = "data.frame"
)

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Data management: annual aggregation
After filtering NA values, we aggregate daily data to annual means per grid cell:

tx <- tx %>%
  filter(!is.na(var)) %>%
  as_tibble()

tx_annual <- tx %>%
  mutate(Time = as_date(Time)) %>%
  mutate(year = year(Time)) %>%
  group_by(year, lon, lat) %>%
  summarise(annual_tx = mean(var)) %>%
  ungroup()

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Trend computation and statistical significance
We apply the Sen’s slope (magnitude of trend per decade) and the Mann-Kendall test (statistical significance) at each grid cell:

tx_trend <- tx_annual %>%
  group_by(lon, lat) %>%
  summarise(
    slope = sens.slope(annual_tx)$estimates * 10,  # per decade
    sign  = mk.test(annual_tx)$p.value
  )

title: Computing trends in a climate grid with Tidyverse
text:
Visualization

ggplot() +
  geom_tile(data = tx_trend, aes(x = lon, y = lat, fill = slope)) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = rev(pals::linearlhot(100)),
    name = "ºC/10y",
    limits = c(0.1, 0.45)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = filter(tx_trend, sign < 0.01),
    aes(x = lon, y = lat, color = "Sig. trend (p < 0.01)"),
    size = 0.4, show.legend = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black"), name = "") +
  coord_fixed(1.3) +
  xlab("Longitude") + ylab("Latitude") +
  labs(
    title = "Decadal trend of maximum temperature in the Balearic Islands",
    subtitle = "(1971–2014)",
    caption = "Database: STEAD (Serrano-Notivoli, 2019)"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  guides(fill = guide_colourbar(barwidth = 9, barheight = 0.5, title.position = "right")) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

The result of running the code above looks like this:



Decadal trend of maximum temperature in the Balearic Islands (1971–2014). Dots indicate statistically significant trends (p < 0.01).
